utterances: (Reading) The garbage being put out and defended by the likes of Malepia Aderro(ph) do more harm than good.
The writers like Relentless and Zane have found a good gig, they're making money, I'm glad for them.
But they shouldn't confuse some anemic prose with being an author of merit.
We appreciate your comments, good or bad.
We want to hear from you.
You can call us at 202-408-3330.
Or you can email us.
Log on to npr.org and click on Contact Us.
And please, please be sure where you're writing from and how to pronounce your name.
This is NPR News.
This is DAY TO DAY from NPR News.
I'm Alex Chadwick.
On this King Day, a lot of people think about race.
Does the color of another person's skin make a difference to you and would you admit it if it did?
Here is DAY TO DAY contributing writer Celia Beasley.
I have a confession to make.
I notice race.
I don't think better or worse of someone because of it.
But if I'm on the bus or walking down the street or in a supermarket, if there's a black person in line with me, I notice.
I notice them more than I notice any other kind of person.
I don't have a negative reaction to their presence.
If anything, I'll find myself being particularly nice or respectful of them.
Like I want to prove to myself and everyone around that not only am I not racist, but that I accept and support a multiracial society.
I'll even go out of my way to smile at a woman's baby or open the door for an elderly black person just to show - I don't know exactly.

It's obviously nicer to open the door for someone than slam it in their face, but the fact that I will do it more often for a black person indicates that I'm still differentiating between them and everyone else.
And I feel a little ridiculous.
I don't want to act around anyone.
I want to react to individuals, not color, and treat them equally because they are all equal.
But obviously, in my eyes they aren't.
Then something happened.
I moved to Madagascar and in Madagascar I am the different one.
I am a vaza, a foreigner, a European.
People notice me.
I get special treatment - sometimes better, sometimes worse - because of how I look.
But to me everyone else is simply Malagasy.
There are many different ethnic groups here and there is tension and history between them, just like in the U.S., but I'm ignorant of all that.
I can't see the subtleties in the way different people react to each other, and so I treat them all the same way.
Last week, I was back in the United States for the first time in a year.
I was standing in line at a supermarket in Oakland and suddenly it hit me.
I wasn't noticing race.
I was behaving as I do in Madagascar and reacting not to race, but to individuals.
I wasn't standing next to a black man in a suit.
I was standing next to man in a suit.
In Madagascar I stand next to black men all the time and it really isn't something worth paying attention to.
Right then it felt like race didn't matter any more than the brand of cereal someone was buying.
It was there but it didn't affect my behavior at all.
But I wonder how long that will last.
What will it be like when I move back to the States for good?
I don't think Americans should ignore race.
It's a big part of our identity and heritage, but I wish it wasn't the first thing I notice about people.
And right now it isn't.
But how can I preserve that?
I don't know if being oblivious to race in Madagascar will be enough to erase decades of noticing race in America.

But it would be nice to think that maybe, just maybe, Madagascar will have changed me, and the next time I'm in line at the supermarket in America I won't notice what kind of person is standing next to me, just the brand of cereal they're buying.
Celia Beasley has been living and writing in Madagascar for the last year.
This is DAY TO DAY.
I'm Alex Chadwick.
And I'm Luke Burbank.
In a few minutes, rescuing frozen embryos from the chaos of Hurricane Katrina.
First this: The Supreme Court heard arguments today about political contributions and teachers' unions.
This is a pair of cases from Washington State, and it could sharply limit the political power of unions.
Dahlia Lithwick is here with us in NPR's D.C.
studios to discuss the case.
She's legal analyst for Slate.com and contributor to DAY TO DAY.
Dahlia, welcome again and help us understand the law at issue here in these Washington State cases.
Sure, Alex.
And it's a little bit complicated.
This is a case about what unions are to do with the nonmembers of the union who must nevertheless pay, quote, ""agency fees"" that go toward collective bargaining activity that the union engages in.
The idea being, there's no free riders.
The problem comes up when some of these agency fees are used by the unions for political activity, not for collective bargaining.

And the state of Washington - by ballot initiative in 1992 - passed a rather unique law that makes the union go to these nonmembers and get, quote, ""affirmative authorization"" from them to use the fees for political speech.

In effect, what Washington said was instead of putting the burden on the nonmembers of the union to opt out of political speech, Washington shifted the burden to the unions and said, go to these nonmembers and ask them to opt in.
So that would be a big thing for the unions to have to do, and the teachers' lawyers are in court.
They're arguing, look, we shouldn't have to do this.
What did they say?
Well, they said, look.
This is not a great, huge free speech case at all.
This is really just a case in which we think that nonmembers - as they said today in argument - shouldn't be asked to opt out twice.
They've already said no to the union.
They've already said, when they declined to join the union, we don't want any part of this.
Why do they have to then go back and go through this whole arduous process of opting out again?
No means no, effectively.
Uh-huh.
And how did the union lawyer respond to this?
Well, what the unions are saying is this is very much a speech case.
This implicates the most cherished form of speech at all - protected under the First Amendment - and that is the right of the union to engage in political advocacy and discourse.
And I'm looking out the window and I'm seeing, like, piles of snow.
Almost the entire detachment here is Kurdish.
Many don't even speak Arabic.
Former peshmerga, as the Kurdish militia men are called, they form the bulk of the Iraqi army in this city now.
Mosul is a mixed city, Sunni Arabs, Shia, Kurds, Yazidis, Christians and Turkmen.
The balance of power is finely calibrated.
The presence of a large number of Kurdish security forces is unwelcomed by some.
Lieutenant Colonel Michael Simmering is with the 3rd Armored Calvary Regiment.

When you had the government stand up, you had a disaffected Sunni Arab population that, quite honestly, because their needs weren't being addressed, sided with the insurgency in hopes that that would eventually bring them back to power.
I think over time, what they're starting to realize is that walking out on the elections in 2005 was something that they will openly admit was a mistake.
The Sunni Arabs boycotted the elections that year, and the result was a Kurdish victory in Mosul.
Thirty-one out of 41 members of the Provincial Counsel are affiliated with Kurdish parties.
The deployment of Kurdish forces here has tipped the balance even further.
Lt.
Col.
SIMMERING: One of the grievances that's listed by the Sunni population is that they perceive the Iraqi army as the Kurdish Peshmerga.
And one only has to enter Sunni Arab areas to see that many people there don't like it.

The mission for Delta Platoon with the 1st Battalion 8th Infantry Regiment was seemingly simple: head out to a residential neighborhood in the eastern part of the city and try and engage the local population.
The military calls it conducting atmospherics.
It's a way of taking the temperature of a new area they may have not patrolled before and getting information.
So this is a really quite neighborhood through here?
A cy-op soldier attached to the unit questions a man sitting in a group by the side of the road.
Unidentified Man #2: (Foreign language spoken)
The man assures the American that there has never been an attack here.
But at the same moment, another resident tells an NPR Iraqi staffer that gunmen came a few days ago and broke the generator that powers the local cell phone tower.
The Americans get back into their vehicles, and not more than 100 feet down the street, they come under fire.
There are no casualties or damage, but Captain Alexander Rasmussen says the message to the population was clear.
When they fire small amounts of, you know, rounds at it, they're not looking to do as much damage to us, but it's trying to intimidate the people is what it's trying to do.
The tactic, though, is effective.
No one wants to talk about the insurgents, but almost everyone has something to say about the Iraqi army.
Twenty-two-year-old Mohad Suivan(ph) is a Kurd living in a predominately Sunni Arab neighborhood.
There's a lot of tensions.
A lot of people talk about it.
Some people hate us, hate Kurds, because, you know, they're the peshmerga.
There's a lot of army are Kurds, so they hate army, especially the Iraqi army.
They're not good with the people.
In another area, the complaints are similar.
The army is more expected in the mostly Sunni Arab police, but people say they are heavy handed.
Mahmoud Unis(ph), a Sunni Arab, works at the central bank in Mussel.
(Through translator) People are scared of the peshmerga because they hurt people.
They break stuff.
They kick whole families out.
But the American soldier who is talking to him doesn't get to hear what Unis is saying.
His translator, a Kurd, instead tells him that Unis is talking about the insurgents who terrorize the neighborhood.
Back at the garrison, Sergeant Major Hussein Awni, a Kurd with the Iraqi army, acknowledges that their presence is sometimes not well-received in Sunni districts of the city.
(Through translator) Even the ones who like us lie when they're asked, they can't say they like us because people are afraid of the terrorists.
Then he turns to brief his men on their next patrol.
Lourdes Garcia-Navarro, NPR News, Mosul.
It's MORNING EDITION from NPR News.
I'm Steve Inskeep.
